summary:
The Mapuche Resistance (Arauco War, c. 1550–1818 AD) was the longest and most successful Indigenous resistance to Spanish colonialism in the Americas — a 270-year conflict in which the Mapuche people of central Chile and Patagonia maintained their independence from the [[event:101|Spanish Empire]] and prevented conquest of their territory through a combination of military effectiveness, rapid tactical adaptation, and fierce attachment to autonomy. Unlike the [[event:2319|Aztec]] and [[event:2320|Inca]] empires, which were conquered quickly by Spanish forces capitalizing on internal divisions and epidemic disease, the Mapuche — a non-centralized people without a single political authority that could be decapitated — adapted to Spanish military methods, adopting horses (acquired from captured Spanish stock) and developing effective cavalry tactics that neutralized the Spanish advantage.

The Spanish established a permanent defensive line (the Bio-Bio River frontier) south of which they accepted de facto Mapuche autonomy — an acknowledgment of military stalemate almost unique in colonial history. The Parliament of Quilín (1641) was a formal peace treaty between the Spanish crown and Mapuche leaders that recognized Mapuche sovereignty south of the Bio-Bio — the only such recognition of Indigenous sovereignty by Spain in the Americas. Mapuche resistance figures, particularly the legendary Lautaro (who had served as Valdivia's personal groom before escaping and organizing resistance), became foundational heroes of Chilean national identity. The Mapuche maintained their autonomy through the colonial period and Chilean independence, only being fully subjugated in the 'Pacification of Araucania' (1861–1883) when the Chilean republic used modern weapons to conquer the remaining Mapuche territory.
